html:
Founder - Grandmaster Liu Yunqiao

Home > Founder - Grandmaster Liu Yunqiao
Growth Background｜Martial Arts Journey｜Martial Arts Spirit

Growth Background

Liu Yunqiao, courtesy name Xiaochen, from the prominent Ji North Head family of Cangnan.

The founding ancestor Liu Yi migrated from Jimo to Cangzhou during the Ming Dynasty. Over generations, twelve family members became officials and scholars; the family was the wealthiest in the local area. By the sixteenth generation, his father Liu Zhiyi was a county scholar; his uncle Liu Zhijie was a stipend scholar, both studied at the Baoding Military Academy and gained reputation in their time. In 1909, on the eighth day of the second lunar month, Liu was born in his hometown, as the seventeenth generation (the County Gazette only recorded up to the fifteenth generation, and the Liu family was not invited to participate in its compilation). He had two elder sisters who unfortunately passed away young, making him the only male heir of the eastern branch. However, he was frail since childhood with a distended abdomen like a drum, worrying his parents. The family bodyguard of his father and grandfather-s generation - Zhang Yaoting, a master of both Taizu and Mizong styles, took special responsibility to care for the young master, applying daily massage techniques and gradually exercising him. When his health improved slightly, he began learning Taizu Long Fist. Liu-s father, being of high status and portly, also practiced this fist as a form of exercise. Sometimes father and son practiced together; during the slow 108-posture routine, Liu-s father would often say: "Yunqiao, when you grow up, you don-t need to become an official or wealthy, just keep your body healthy and continue the Liu family lineage - all our ancestors will be grateful to you!" He then continued with Mizong style, and his health improved further. Liu-s father was overjoyed and, since Zhang was also their tenant farmer, gifted him all the farmland with generous retirement funds before sending him home.

Simultaneously, Liu-s father searched extensively, determined to find the best masters to teach his beloved son. Thus, with a large carriage and humble demeanor, he welcomed the "Divine Spear" Li Shuwen to live and eat together with Liu, specializing in Baji and Pigua arts. After three years of pure training with rapid progress and fierce dedication, they had only completed one small Baji set. Liu hosted a banquet, and after drinking, cautiously inquired about this slow progress. Li glared angrily and said: "Don-t I know what he should practice?" The entire gathering fell into awkward silence.

Li-s skills were exceptionally refined but his temper was extraordinary. Throughout his life, countless opponents were injured in his hands. He once served as a tutor for Governor Li Jinglin but offended him and was sent away. Li harbored resentment and sought revenge. Jinglin was famous for his sword skills, earning the reputation "Number One Sword Under Heaven." Li often disparaged him: "Wearing official robes to practice sword, and he can-t even do it properly - one punch would kill him!" In 1931, after Liu completed his training, Li took him to tour Shandong, believing that to advance his skills, real combat was the only true method. He also sought opportunities to humiliate Jinglin. Being from a prominent family, Liu was related to Feng Yuxiang, Zhang Ji, etc., and was well-acquainted with many in the Beiyang Army and Republican official circles, providing him protection.

Arriving in Huang County, they stayed at the headquarters of Li-s disciple, General Zhang Xiangwu, commander of the Fifth Route Army. Xiangwu was actually friendly with Jinglin and often mediated on their behalf. Liu went out daily to visit friends and test skills, proving unbeatable in both empty-hand and weapons combat, earning the nickname "Little Overlord." One morning, exiting the back gate of the headquarters led to the Ding Family Garden-s donated Public Education Hall, where he saw an elderly man "misleading students" in the martial arts class. Liu challenged him and was unexpectedly knocked down by a single "Spreading Palm" technique. Xiangwu learned it was the Six Harmony Praying Mantis master Ding Zicheng, and arranged for Ding to teach this reckless junior brother the Mantis techniques.

During this period, he also met Xiangwu-s Bagua teacher - Gong Baotian. After briefly trying it, he deeply enjoyed it and followed Gong to Yantai to study deeply at his North Mountain residence. Xiangwu investigated drug trafficking and suppressed bandits, killing many; locals even called him "Zhang King of Hell" and "Zhang Skin-flayer." Confiscated opium was used as tuition fees for the junior brother, offered to Gong for enjoyment. Liu-s father also sent monthly silver dollars from home as additional tuition. Thus, Liu studied the divine Yin-Yang Bagua palms with "black and white" tuition fees, which could be considered a remarkable encounter!

Li, feeling bored and lonely throughout the days, bid farewell to Xiangwu-s protection and returned home alone. Passing through Weixian County, hearing of his great reputation, people sought combat and he killed another opponent. Pretending to respect him, they hosted him but eventually poisoned him! The peerless master met his end in 1934. Xiangwu notified Liu, but neither revenge nor capturing the murderer was possible. Disheartened, Liu decided to return home.

In 1936, among Japanese officers in the Tianjin concession was Ota Tokusaburo, a high-ranking kendo master. He consistently belittled Chinese martial arts as useless, considering sword techniques particularly flowery, like dancing, unable to withstand a single blow. He publicly challenged others but found no takers, growing more arrogant thinking China had no capable practitioners. Liu heard this and angrily took the night train to Tianjin, arranging a public sword match at French Park. Tianjin-s martial arts community was excited and came to challenge. At the appointed time, both sides fought with wooden swords. Ota charged directly; Liu countered with a side attack using "One Sword Transforming Three Shadows," striking his ribs consecutively. Ota dropped his sword, clutching his chest in surrender.

Thus, Tianjin-s martial arts community treated him well. Meeting senior brother Huo Diankui, Diankui was surprised that their Baji and Pigua from the same teacher differed significantly. Becoming acquainted with Bagua master Sun Xikun, just as Gong-s letter arrived, Xikun was shocked to learn Liu was also a Bagua practitioner. Meeting Shanxi-s Ren Dekui, he found his Xingyi impressive. Wang Shusen from Zhaobao dominated with Chen-style Taijiquan. Fellow townsman Li Yuxiang from Caryuan was renowned in Tianjin for his Green Duckweed Sword. Liu particularly loved the "Cun Ba Fan" of Wang Yunzhang, director of 78 martial arts halls in Liangjia District, wanting to learn from him. Yunzhang looked down on him and was disrespectful. Liu seized the opportunity to severely beat him before his disciples, then cursed and stormed out. These were the major events of his youthful prime, around ages 15-20.

Liu-s father believed martial arts should aim for health, but his beloved son was too immersed. As he grew older, he should focus on proper career. Thus, in 1937 he entered Chaoyang University to study law. Unexpectedly, the Marco Polo Bridge Incident erupted, beginning full-scale war. Liu escaped to the northwest, entering Xi-an Military Branch School to study military strategy.

Martial Arts Journey

After graduation, he joined the army starting as platoon leader, repeatedly distinguishing himself in battle, rising to company, battalion, and regiment commander, reaching colonel rank. He was wounded several times, once captured and taken to Yuncheng. Expecting death, he showed no fear. Japanese soldiers enjoyed fighting prisoners for sport and practical training. Liu consistently defeated them overwhelmingly. The Japanese respected his courage and skill, even sharing their military ration canned meats. Guards relaxed slightly, and Liu escaped over the wall at night. Many followed attempting escape! Only gunfire like rain was heard - poor souls perished under bullets!

Liu led troops into Shaanxi, meeting Tianjin acquaintance Li Zengshu from Guanyun in Xi-an, then working as bodyguard for Northwest Farmers Bank. Zengshu long resided in Shanxi with pure Xingyi skills, excelling in spear, seeking a match. They fought intensely for some time until Zengshu finally yielded to Liu-s exceptional left-hand spear technique. In Baoji, he met former Kaifeng Martial Arts Hall director Ma Jinyi, extremely skilled in Xinyi Liuhe. Discussing martial arts in a teahouse, disagreements arose and they tested hands. Jinyi aggressively attacked the middle section; Liu used a Baji technique "Supporting the Window" to redirect force, throwing him over the tea table. Jinyi sighed in admiration and personally delivered a consultant appointment letter the next day, beginning a deep friendship.

In 1949, he followed the army to Taiwan. Served as paratroop battalion commander, soaring through blue skies with undiminished spirit. Later transferred to Ministry of National Defense Personnel Office, evaluating ranks of military personnel in Taiwan. Finding desk work tedious and unsuitable, he moved to Joint Service Headquarters, supervising military dependents- villages, providing shelter for military families. Liu remained清廉自守 (clean and self-disciplined), living modestly in Taipei suburbs, Jingmei Town, in three sugarcane-board partitioned rooms of a former Japanese-era market converted to temporary military housing, with a small courtyard. Before reaching fifty, he resigned with感慨 (deep feeling).

Taipei-s New Park was a famous training ground. Many masters taught there. The only Six Harmony Praying Mantis inheritor in Taiwan - Zhang Xiangsan, taught here consistently for over thirty years. Around 1966, chatting with two non-martial artists who came for exercise, one said: "Our Cangzhou Liu Yunqiao, how about..." while another said: "Should be our Tianjin Liu Yunqiao, what about..." Xiangsan smiled, saying "I-m afraid he-s my junior brother, our Shandong Huang County-s Liu Yunqiao!" Among the three, only the Cangzhou man was actually from the same hometown and knew Liu was in Taiwan, introducing him to Xiangsan. Their handshake felt like reuniting after lifetimes.

Thus he could no longer remain hidden and began interacting with martial arts circles. At that time, the Legislative Yuan hosted weekly martial arts gatherings every Sunday. One meeting room was for Taijiquan Society activities, while the Republic Hall gathered masters of various styles, jokingly called "Gathering Righteousness Hall." Contemporary opinion held: "Taiwan-s martial arts are strongest in Taipei; Taipei-s top masters all -hold meetings- at the Legislative Yuan."

Interacting with these giants, Liu felt怅然若有所失 (melancholy and somewhat lost)... He then accepted three to five students, applying severe training. After guidance, within months they transformed completely, astonishing everyone who increasingly urged him to participate in martial arts organizations and activities.

There was a National Combat Committee chaired by General Hu Weike. Meeting Liu, he was greatly surprised; he sought meetings every few days for enjoyment. He also introduced General Chiang Wei-kuo, who exclaimed: "Is this the Liu Yunqiao who defended Tongguan with me?" In 1968, first undertaking major responsibility, replacing Weike as deputy leader of the Chinese Martial Arts Team, leading elite troops to visit Malaysia. Comforting overseas Chinese, exchanging with martial arts circles, and fundraising for Malaysia-s Social Research Institute, holding grand charity performances in Kuala Lumpur with great success.

In 1970, invited to the Philippines. Opened Taijiquan, Bagua Zhang, and Kunwu Sword classes in Manila. Due to Philippine floods, participated in charity performances for disaster relief.

In June 1971, founded "Wutan" martial arts magazine. Despite positive reviews, management skills were lacking! By 1973, funds depleted and it closed.

However, the magazine-s attached martial arts training class grew strong; continuing as Wutan Martial Arts Promotion Center. Its coaches now teach at over twenty university martial arts clubs island-wide. As students were mostly educated youth, many studied or worked abroad, thus Wutan disciples spread across Asia, America, and Europe, including many in high positions. This significantly elevated martial arts- status and changed public perception.

Japanese martial artists, hearing Liu-s reputation, visited Taiwan for instruction, realizing the vastness of martial arts. After mainland China opened, motivation grew to continue studying Baji and Pigua, maintaining persistent enthusiasm. Not just Cangzhou; also Beijing, Shanghai; even northwest and northeast Baji masters were visited for instruction or invited to Japan. According to Japanese statistics, besides Chen-style Taijiquan, Baji is the most popular style. Tracing origins, Liu actually initiated this trend.

At disciples- request, Liu visited the US in 1982 and Japan in 1983. Now aged eighty with three thousand disciples, he still works hard expanding martial arts. In Taipei, he personally directs Wutan Center training without rest. Annual winter and summer trainings in central (Puli), south (Guanziling Daxian Temple), and north (Jiaobanshan Scenic Area) regions remain major martial arts events attracting island-wide attention.

Martial Arts Spirit

Examining Liu-s martial arts comprehensively: except for Yang-style Taijiquan and Six Harmony Praying Mantis (which he learned but not deeply due to temperament mismatch, better not discussed), his Taizu Long Fist has many slow postures, called "supporting slow fist"; movements are古朴深沉饱满 (simple, deep, and full), closely resembling Qi Jiguang-s Boxing Classic illustrations; differing from the 50-70 varieties of Taizu Long Fist in Zhili, Shandong, and Henan.

His Mizong includes three sets. First set "Mizong Frame" has paired practice; second set "Ambush from Ten Sides" also has paired practice; third set comprises single techniques for practical application. This greatly differs from various Mizong and Yanqing styles in Hebei, Shandong, etc.

Regarding his Baji and Pigua, he completely inherited Divine Spear Li-s late-period knowledge. Not only differing from other lineages- techniques, but also varying from Li-s early and middle-period teachings. These differences reveal Li-s evolutionary process and the essence of his art.

Bagua palm methods vary between schools. The Shandong Gong lineage was almost completely transmitted to Liu - Gong taught according to individual students without absolute sequence. Its excellence lies in having levels, methods, gradual guidance leading to mastery. Thus Liu-s Bagua includes progressive routines like climbing stairs, plus basics, training methods, auxiliary equipment, and unique Bagua weapons.

Liu-s sword foundation began with Taizu-s Night Battle Sword in childhood, reaching peak skill with Pigua Sword. With fellow villagers, he carved wooden swords to practice guard and hilt techniques. Today, few can truly utilize Willow Leaf Sword-s characteristics without confusing it with other sword styles. The beechwood sword Liu used for teaching, with raw rubber guard, preserves Willow Leaf Sword-s true essence.

People today know Jinglin-s divine sword skills were profound, but don-t realize they originated from Kunwu techniques. Jinglin promoted martial arts but kept Kunwu Sword secret throughout life. Liu initially learned this from Xiangwu and enjoyed it. Comparing with Wu Shu-s "Sword Formula" poem in "Arm Record," it matched perfectly, confirming ancient origins. But Li despised it, thinking: "How could an official like Jinglin have real training? Without foundation, it-s empty talk." He had Xiangwu test swords, often disarming him instantly. Then personally analyzed for Liu, keeping Kunwu routines but adding power emission methods to each posture. This enhanced Liu-s sword skills tremendously. With single techniques, routines, applications, and power methods; Chinese sword transmission gained complete preservation.

Liu often said: "The name -Baji- was probably coined by scholarly types. Back home, locals called it -rake fist- because empty-handed techniques resemble farm tools. Qi Jiguang-s Boxing Classic records -Bazi Fist and Staff- - exactly this. Liu-s Baji staff method uses eight-foot poles, holding and threading without set routines. First practice solo, then paired combat. Every technique is practical, techniques within techniques - ancient battlefield formations."

The great spear was Li-s proudest skill, which Liu truly inherited. Each morning-s teaching began with basic single techniques, progressing to small twists and wheel movements. Arms bruised purple, skin never healed year-round! Thus Liu-s spear skills became exquisite beyond comparison. Attacking, he followed the shaft deeply; defending, he divided spears upon contact. Not only mastering this weapon, he incorporated spear methods into other weapons, making all superb. "Spear is ancestor of hundred weapons" finds proof here.

Liu-s martial arts writings appear scattered in "Wutan" and other magazines, uncollected. His book "Baji Quan" has Chinese editions in Taiwan/Hong Kong and Japanese edition in Tokyo. New work "Kunwu Sword" in two volumes is completed. "Pigua Zhang" is being proofread. Disciples- recorded teachings have accumulated volumes, currently being categorized and organized systematically.
